edges.description: PHF6 dysfunction perturbs direct transcriptional targets involved in neural stem cell control.
Disrupted transcription alters downstream neuronal growth and migration programs.
Loss of PHF6-dependent Ephrin receptor expression perturbs embryonic neural stem cell behavior.
Early neural stem cell dysregulation contributes to later cognitive impairment.
Abnormal neural precursor regulation contributes to downstream cortical dysfunction and seizure susceptibility.
Impaired neuronal development contributes to the core cognitive phenotype.

nodes.description: PHF6 loss impairs neuron proliferation, neurite extension, and migration in neuron-like cells.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of PHF6 leads to aberrant development of human neuron-like cells
evidence.snippet: Subsequently, we could demonstrate that PHF6 is indeed required for proper neuron proliferation, neurite outgrowth and migration.

nodes.description: PHF6 normally promotes Ephrin receptor expression in the developing brain. This regulatory step is impaired in BFLS models.

evidence.reference_title: PHF6-mediated transcriptional control of NSC via Ephrin receptors is impaired in the intellectual disability syndrome BFLS
evidence.snippet: We identify a panel of Ephrin receptors (EphRs) as direct transcriptional targets of PHF6.

nodes.description: BFLS model systems show abnormal embryonic neural stem cell behavior with dysregulated self-renewal and altered neural progenitor output.

evidence.reference_title: PHF6-mediated transcriptional control of NSC via Ephrin receptors is impaired in the intellectual disability syndrome BFLS
evidence.snippet: Characterization of BFLS mice harbouring PHF6 patient mutations reveals an increase in embryonic neural stem cell (eNSC) self-renewal and a reduction of neural progenitors.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of PHF6 causes spontaneous seizures, enlarged brain ventricles and altered transcription in the cortex of a mouse model of the Börjeson–Forssman–Lehmann intellectual disability syndrome
evidence.snippet: Phf6 deficient neural precursor cells showed a reduced capacity for self-renewal and increased differentiation into neurons.

nodes.description: Intellectual disability is a defining core feature of BFLS.

evidence.reference_title: Börjeson–Forssman–Lehmann syndrome: delineating the clinical and allelic spectrum in 14 new families
evidence.snippet: AbstractBörjeson-Forssman-Lehmann syndrome (BFLS) is an X-linked intellectual disability syndrome caused by variants in the PHF6 gene.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of PHF6 leads to aberrant development of human neuron-like cells
evidence.snippet: Pathogenic variants in PHD finger protein 6 (PHF6) cause Borjeson–Forssman–Lehmann syndrome (BFLS), a rare X-linked neurodevelopmental disorder, which manifests variably in both males and females.

evidence.reference_title: Börjeson–Forssman–Lehmann syndrome: delineating the clinical and allelic spectrum in 14 new families
evidence.snippet: Affected males often have missense variants or small in-frame deletions while affected females tend to have truncating variants or large deletions/duplications.
PHF6 | HGNC:18145 | Borjeson-Forssman-Lehmann syndrome | MONDO:0010537 | XL | Definitive

nodes.description: PHF6 is a transcriptional regulator. Loss of PHF6 function disrupts transcriptional programs required for normal brain development in BFLS.

biological_processes: regulation of transcription by RNA polymerase II

evidence.reference_title: PHF6-mediated transcriptional control of NSC via Ephrin receptors is impaired in the intellectual disability syndrome BFLS
evidence.snippet: The plant homeodomain zinc-finger protein, PHF6, is a transcriptional regulator, and PHF6 germline mutations cause the X-linked intellectual disability (XLID) Börjeson-Forssman-Lehmann syndrome (BFLS).

nodes.description: Seizures occur in BFLS model systems and support a seizure-prone neurologic phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of PHF6 causes spontaneous seizures, enlarged brain ventricles and altered transcription in the cortex of a mouse model of the Börjeson–Forssman–Lehmann intellectual disability syndrome
evidence.snippet: We show that loss of PHF6 resulted in spontaneous seizures occurring via a neural intrinsic mechanism.
